eli3: AdaBoost — Adaptive Boosting — trains a sequence of weak classifiers (barely better than random guessing), where each new classifier focuses harder on the examples that previous classifiers got wrong.

After each round, misclassified examples have their weights increased and correctly classified examples have their weights decreased. The next weak learner must pay more attention to the hard cases. The final prediction is a weighted vote of all the weak learners, where more accurate learners get louder votes.

AdaBoost was the first practical boosting algorithm and proved that combining many weak learners can produce an arbitrarily strong learner. It was a landmark result in machine learning theory and the ancestor of modern gradient boosting.

whenWhy.title: Binary classification with weak learners
whenWhy.body: AdaBoost works well with very simple base learners — decision stumps (1-level trees) are the classic choice. The ensemble of stumps is fast to train, produces a sparse model, and often achieves competitive accuracy on simple classification tasks.

whenWhy.title: Understanding boosting theory
whenWhy.body: AdaBoost is the canonical example for understanding boosting theory: the exponential loss connection, the relationship between training error and ensemble complexity, and the PAC learning framework. Understanding AdaBoost is essential foundation for gradient boosting.

whenWhy.title: Face detection (Viola-Jones)
whenWhy.body: The Viola-Jones face detector (2001) — which dominated real-time face detection for a decade — is an AdaBoost classifier over simple Haar-like features. AdaBoost's ability to select informative features automatically made it ideal for high-dimensional visual tasks.

whenWhy.title: When training data is noisy — be cautious
whenWhy.body: AdaBoost exponentially upweights misclassified examples. If those examples are mislabelled noise, AdaBoost will fixate on them and overfit badly. It is highly sensitive to label noise. For noisy data, prefer gradient boosting with shrinkage or Random Forest.

deepDiveIntro: AdaBoost (Freund & Schapire, 1997) trains a sequence of weak classifiers on reweighted versions of the training data. After each round, misclassified examples get higher weights and correctly classified ones get lower weights. The final model is a weighted majority vote across all the weak classifiers.

AdaBoost can be understood as minimising an exponential loss function — the penalty for misclassified examples grows exponentially the more confidently they are misclassified. This connection, established by Friedman et al. (2000), unified AdaBoost with the broader gradient boosting framework.

deepDive.heading: The AdaBoost Algorithm
deepDive.body: Start every training example with equal weight. In each round, fit a weak classifier on the weighted data, then compute how accurate it was. Better classifiers earn a higher vote weight. Misclassified examples get their weights boosted so the next classifier pays more attention to them, while correctly classified examples get their weights reduced. Repeat for M rounds, then take a weighted vote of all classifiers. [1]
deepDive.formula: αₘ = ½ log((1 − εₘ) / εₘ),    wᵢ ← wᵢ · exp(−αₘ yᵢ hₘ(xᵢ))

deepDive.heading: Learner Weight
deepDive.body: A perfect classifier gets a very high vote weight; a random-guessing classifier gets zero weight. A classifier that is worse than random actually gets a negative weight — its predictions are flipped, which is still useful. AdaBoost only requires each weak learner to do slightly better than random guessing to guarantee overall improvement. [1]

deepDive.heading: Training Error Bound
deepDive.body: AdaBoost comes with a theoretical guarantee: as long as each weak learner does just a little better than random guessing, the training error of the final ensemble drops exponentially as you add more rounds. Add enough rounds and the training error can be driven to zero. [1][2]

deepDive.heading: Connection to Exponential Loss
deepDive.body: AdaBoost can be interpreted as greedily minimising an exponential loss: examples that are confidently misclassified pay an exponentially large penalty. The sample weights AdaBoost maintains at each round are exactly these per-example losses, which is why fitting a new classifier on the current weights is equivalent to taking a gradient step. [1]

deepDive.heading: Sensitivity to Outliers & Noise
deepDive.body: The exponential penalty grows much faster than a log penalty for wrong predictions. Points that are always misclassified — whether they are genuinely hard, mislabelled, or just noise — get exponentially increasing weights, and the model fixates on them. This is AdaBoost's double-edged sword: it focuses on hard cases, but also overfits badly to noisy labels. [1]

takeaways: AdaBoost reweights training examples after each round — misclassified examples get upweighted exponentially. [1]
Learner weight αₘ = ½ log((1−εₘ)/εₘ): better classifiers get louder votes in the final ensemble. [1]
Training error is bounded by exp(−2Σγₘ²) — decreases exponentially as long as each learner beats random. [1]
AdaBoost minimises exponential loss via forward stagewise additive modelling. [1]
Exponential loss makes AdaBoost highly sensitive to mislabelled data — prefer GBM with shrinkage for noisy problems. [1]
Decision stumps (depth-1 trees) are the canonical weak learner for AdaBoost. [1][2]

edgeCases.q: What makes a 'weak learner' in practice?
edgeCases.a: Formally, a weak learner need only do slightly better than random (error < 0.5 on weighted training data). In practice, decision stumps (a single threshold on one feature) are the standard choice — they are simple, fast, and easy to fit optimally on weighted data by scanning all feature thresholds. Any other classifier that avoids overfitting on weighted data (logistic regression, linear SVM) also qualifies. [1]

edgeCases.q: How does AdaBoost relate to Gradient Boosting?
edgeCases.a: Gradient Boosting (Friedman, 2001) generalises AdaBoost. AdaBoost = Gradient Boosting with exponential loss and hard classification output. By replacing exponential loss with other differentiable losses (MSE, log-loss) and allowing real-valued weak learners (regression trees instead of ±1 classifiers), you get the full Gradient Boosting framework. [1]

edgeCases.q: Why doesn't AdaBoost seem to overfit even with many rounds?
edgeCases.a: Empirically, AdaBoost's test error often continues to decrease even after training error reaches zero — a puzzle at the time of its discovery. The explanation: additional rounds increase the margin (confidence) of correct predictions even after accuracy plateaus. The margin theory (Schapire et al., 1998) shows that larger margins → better generalisation bounds. This inspired the development of SVMs. [1][2]

The original AdaBoost is binary (y ∈ {−1,+1}). Multi-class extensions include AdaBoost.M1 (one-vs-all with weak learner error < 1−1/K), AdaBoost.M2, and SAMME (Stagewise Additive Modelling using a Multi-class Exponential loss) — which is what scikit-learn implements as its default multi-class strategy. [1]

sources.label: Freund & Schapire — A Decision-Theoretic Generalization of On-Line Learning (1997)
Hastie, Tibshirani & Friedman — The Elements of Statistical Learning, Ch. 10
